results.possibleIdentity: David Abramson is an American experimental filmmaker and artist who was active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. In 1968, at the age of 28, he was awarded one of the inaugural Independent Filmmaker grants from the American Film Institute (AFI) to produce a film exploring dramatic and documentary styles. His work from this period includes the 16mm black-and-white experimental film "Girl On a Landscape" (1968), which dealt with themes of psychology and mental health. His films have been distributed by The Film-Makers' Cooperative in New York. Given his activity in the New York experimental art and film community during the late 1960s, he is a plausible associate of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted similar interdisciplinary collaborations in the city at that time.

Professor David Abramson (born c. 1954) is a distinguished Australian computer scientist and engineer who has combined a career in high-performance computing and software engineering with artistic pursuits. He has been active in technological research since 1979, holding senior positions at Monash University and the University of Queensland. In addition to his scientific contributions, Abramson is a creative artist whose works include the play "Purely Academic" (first performed/read in the 2010s), which explores the ethics of scientific research. He is also known to engage in photography and stained-glass window making. His identity as a scientist-engineer who creates art aligns with the core mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to foster collaborations between those in the technical and artistic fields, although his primary professional activity began slightly later than the late 1960s.
